abstract: The global water crisis—encompassing pollution, overextraction, ecosystem collapse, and inequitable access—persists despite decades of infrastructure investment and efficiency improvements. Progress toward Sustainable Development Goal 6 remains insufficient, largely due to policy inertia, regulatory capture, and a narrow focus on economic growth. This paper proposes a "Beyond Growth" framing to rethink modern water responses. Grounded in systems thinking, justice principles, and relationality, this perspective prioritizes the needs of the most marginalized and advocates for the decoupling of economic development from environmental harm. By shifting from traditional "build and grow" models toward justice-principled institutional innovations and inclusive governance, a Beyond Growth approach offers a transformative pathway toward a sustainable and equitable water future.
